task_context.question: Who's album which reflects on his depressive state, had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976?
task_context.agent_role: Produce one compact second-hop retrieval query targeting the missing bridge or supporting evidence.

visible_agent_inputs.question: Who's album which reflects on his depressive state, had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976?
visible_agent_inputs.summary_1: Evidence: passage [4] — "as a song from his 1976 live album 'Frampton Comes Alive!' . The song reached number 6 on the 'Billboard' Hot 100" (Peter Frampton); passage [6] — song "recorded ... for his 1976 album 'A Night on the Town'. The song became his second US chart topper on the 'Billboard' Hot 100, spending 8 weeks at #1 from November 13, 1976 - January 1, 1977" (Rod Stewart). Missing bridge: which album (and which artist) is described as "reflects on his depressive state." Next retrieval should find the album/artist described as reflecting on his depressive state and then determine whether that artist/album corresponds to one of the 1976 Hot 100 hits above.

current_output.query: album reflects on his depressive state Rod Stewart Peter Frampton A Night on the Town Frampton Comes Alive!

input_dict.current_prompt:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.

output.query: album reflects on his depressive state Rod Stewart Peter Frampton A Night on the Town Frampton Comes Alive!

retrieved_titles: Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song)
retrieved_doc_snippets: Frampton Comes Alive! II | Frampton Comes Alive! II is a 1995 live album by English musician Peter Frampton. It is a sequel to his 1976 multiplatinum album "Frampton Comes Alive". The title is styled as Frampton Comes Alive II on packaging.

Frampton Comes Alive! | Frampton Comes Alive! is a double live album by English rock musician Peter Frampton released in 1976. It is one of the best-selling live albums in the United States, and considered by many to be one of the finest live rock recordings of all time. Following four solo albums with little commercial success, "Frampton Comes Alive!" was a breakthrough for the artist.

Frampton (album) | Frampton is the fourth studio album by Peter Frampton, released in 1975 before he went on tour and recorded "Frampton Comes Alive! " The most recognizable songs from the album are "Show Me the Way" and "Baby, I Love Your Way" which became big hits when released as singles from "Frampton Comes Alive!" The album peaked at #32 on the "Billboard" 200.

Live in San Francisco, March 24, 1975 | Peter Frampton, Live In San Francisco, March 24, 1975 is a live recording by Peter Frampton and the precursor to the Record Breaking "Frampton Comes Alive." It's a much more intimate show, with a smaller audience, broadcast on San Francisco's KSAN from Sausalito's Record Plant, literally only months before he recorded the Live album, "Frampton Comes Alive," that would break records as one of the best-selling live albums in the United States.

Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song) | "Show Me the Way" is a song written by Peter Frampton, which was originally released on his 1975 album "Frampton" and as a single, but gained its highest popularity as a song from his 1976 live album "Frampton Comes Alive! ". The song reached number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and number 10 on the UK Singles Chart, becoming his biggest hit until "I'm in You" in 1977.

Hiatt Comes Alive at Budokan? | Hiatt Comes Alive at Budokan? was singer-songwriter John Hiatt's twelfth album and first live album, released in 1994. The album was actually recorded at venues in North America; the title parodies both Peter Frampton's "Frampton Comes Alive! " and the "At Budokan" albums released by numerous artists.

Peter Frampton | Peter Kenneth Frampton (born 22 April 1950) is an English rock musician, singer, songwriter, producer, and guitarist. He was previously associated with the bands Humble Pie and The Herd. At the end of his 'group' career was Frampton's international breakthrough album his live release, "Frampton Comes Alive! " The album sold in the United States more than 8 million copies and spawned several single hits. Since then he has released several major albums. He has also worked with Dav ...[truncated]

One single by the band, "Sweet Summer Music" reached #94 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 in 1976. James Taylor later covered their single "Honey Don't Leave L.A." and th ...[truncated]

Honey Don't Leave L.A. | "Honey Don't Leave L.A." is a song written by Danny Kortchmar. It was first recorded by Attitudes, a band consisting of Kortchmar, Paul Stallworth, Jim Keltner and David Foster, who met while backing George Harrison on his album "Extra Texture (Read All About It)". It was released on Attitudes' self-titled debut album in 1976. It was also released as a promotional single on Harrison's Dark Horse Records label.

Extra Texture (Read All About It) | Extra Texture (Read All About It) is the sixth studio album by English musician George Harrison, released in September 1975. It was Harrison's final album under his contract with Apple Records and EMI, and the last studio album issued by Apple. The release came nine months after his troubled 1974 North American tour with Ravi Shankar and the poorly received "Dark Horse" album. The melancholic mood of the recording reflects Harrison's depressed state at the cri ...[truncated]

You (George Harrison song) | "You" is a song by English musician George Harrison, released as the opening track of his 1975 album "Extra Texture (Read All About It)". It was also the album's lead single, becoming a top 20 hit in America and reaching number 9 in Canada. A 45-second instrumental portion of the song, titled "A Bit More of You", appears on "Extra Texture" also, opening side two of the original LP format. Harrison wrote "You" in 1970 as a song for Ronnie Spector, formerly of the Rone ...[truncated]

Tired of Midnight Blue | "Tired of Midnight Blue" is a song by English musician George Harrison, released on his 1975 album "Extra Texture (Read All About It)". It was written after a night out with music-industry executives in Los Angeles – an event that Harrison found particularly depressing. The recording includes contributions from Leon Russell, on piano, and Jim Keltner, who plays drums and percussion.

Can't Stop Thinking About You | "Can't Stop Thinking About You" is a song by English musician George Harrison, released in 1975 on his final album for Apple Records, "Extra Texture (Read All About It)". A love song in the style of a soul/R&B ballad, it was written by Harrison in December 1973, towards the end of his marriage to Pattie Boyd and while he was having an affair with Maureen Starkey, the wife of his former Beatles bandmate Ringo Starr. Having first considered the song for his 1974 rel ...[truncated]

World of Stone | "World of Stone" is a song by English rock musician George Harrison, released in 1975 on "Extra Texture (Read All About It)", his final album for Apple Records. It was also issued as the B-side of the album's lead single, "You". Harrison wrote the song in 1973 but recorded it two years later, following the unfavourable critical reception afforded his 1974 North American tour and the "Dark Horse" album. Due to its context on release, commentators view "World of Stone" as a plea f ...[truncated]
